frameworks.name: moral dilemma paradigm
frameworks.one_line: Classic single-decision moral dilemmas extended to two-stage sequential decisions.

frameworks.name: utilitarian vs deontological choice
frameworks.one_line: Choice between harming one to save many versus refusing harm.

frameworks.name: consistency in sequential choice
frameworks.one_line: Tendency to stay consistent with one's prior decision within a narrative.

core_question: How does a prior moral decision within the same narrative influence a subsequent moral decision?
key_assumptions: Moral dilemmas can be operationalized as utilitarian vs deontological choices
Two-stage scenarios reveal effects unobservable in single-decision paradigms
Choice consistency reflects internal commitment to one's first decision
manipulations: Type of first action (utilitarian vs deontological)
Presence of a prior decision within the same narrative
key_findings: Second decisions were mostly consistent with first decisions
About 29% of cases showed switching (inconsistent responding)
Having made a prior decision decreased utilitarian responding overall
interpretations: Prior choices within the same setting significantly shape later moral decisions
Sequential narrative context modulates the utilitarian/deontological balance
